fahim_mougamadou.name: Fahim Mougamadou
fahim_mougamadou.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Fahim Mougamadou is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 11 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Fahim competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

In doubles, Fahim has formed effective partnerships:
  • Adithya S Burisetty (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #5)

Fahim has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Fahim isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
fahim_mougamadou.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Fahim Mougamadou, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Fahim's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Fahim keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Fahim saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Fahim plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Adithya S Burisetty: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Adithya S Burisetty has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U15 — we hope Adithya S Burisetty has emotionally recovered.

Fahim has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Fahim has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Fahim showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Fahim traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Fahim entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Fahim paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Fahim is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Fahim wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

felicia_ho.name: Felicia Ho
felicia_ho.summary:
Hailing from California, Felicia Ho has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Felicia's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Felicia has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Felicia competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 27 different doubles partners over the years, Felicia adapts to anyone:
  • Daniel Bicheng Fong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Maximus Nguyen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #3)
  • Shivani Ramakrishnan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Celine Ao (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Felicia has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Felicia has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Felicia consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Felicia brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
felicia_ho.roast:
Allow us to introduce Felicia Ho, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 94 career entries in 34 tournaments, Felicia has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 93 entries where Felicia drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 94 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Felicia saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Can we talk about Felicia and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Felicia can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Felicia still cannot reach it. Girls' Doubles finals and Felicia — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Felicia has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Felicia's trophies to save time.

If Felicia's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Felicia has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC, where Felicia entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Felicia has ZERO titles. None. 5 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Felicia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 94 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 27 of them, to be precise. Felicia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Felicia's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Phillip You: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Phillip You has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ahan Senthilkumar has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Felicia entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Felicia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Felicia and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Felicia has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Felicia a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Felicia has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2022 SoCal CRC in GS U13, where Felicia was seeded #2 and finished 9th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Felicia has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Felicia is not just losing locally — Felicia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ho family travels cross-country so Felicia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Felicia has 42 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Felicia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Felicia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Felicia entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Felicia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 94 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Felicia. Felicia just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Felicia has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Felicia. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Felicia: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

2024-2025.t: 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC

felix_zhang.name: Felix Zhang

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Felix adapts to anyone:
  • Vedaant Sreedharan (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in BD, spanning 5 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Felix has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Felix has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Felix has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
felix_zhang.roast:
Allow us to introduce Felix Zhang, from Missouri, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Felix has 20 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Felix saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Felix's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 5 entries — a stretch where Felix actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Felix is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Felix plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Felix is 0-for-8. Zero titles in 8 tries. The best singles result is a 17th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Felix and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Felix needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Felix has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Vedaant Sreedharan (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Bella Hu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Felix's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Felix has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Felix of is playing it safe. Felix has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Felix has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Felix has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Felix is not just losing locally — Felix is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Felix can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Felix has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Felix showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Felix traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Felix entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Felix paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Felix story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Felix comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Felix, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

fonghong_lin.roast:
Allow us to introduce Fonghong Lin, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Fonghong has 49 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Fonghong saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Fonghong's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

In Boys' Doubles, Fonghong has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Fonghong chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Fonghong's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Fonghong has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, where Fonghong entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Fonghong does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Fonghong has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 1 wins means Fonghong is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Fonghong plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Fonghong is 0-for-17. Zero titles in 17 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Fonghong and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Fonghong needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Fonghong has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Fonghong's texts. The go-to partner is Ayaan Kale (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Aanya Latne has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Fonghong's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. Junior Nationals has seen Fonghong 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Fonghong is not just losing locally — Fonghong is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Fonghong can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Fonghong has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Fonghong showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11, which means Fonghong traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Fonghong entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Fonghong paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Fonghong's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 48 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Fonghong is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. Will Fonghong figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Fonghong's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

francesca_corbett.roast:

That is genuinely impressive, and Francesca's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 13 career entries to discuss and a 77% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Francesca saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior, where Francesca burst onto the scene with a title in GS U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Francesca has delivered on some of those promises: 10 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Francesca promised and what Francesca has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2016-2017 season — 3 titles in 3 entries — a stretch where Francesca actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 2 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Francesca plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 5 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Francesca can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

Francesca has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Francesca has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Francesca showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 GS, which means Francesca traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 U.S. Selection Event in U19 GS. A pattern, not a blip.

The Francesca story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 10 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. But every season Francesca comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Francesca, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.
